name: In Her Interest by Zerodha - Delhi
description: Women have a complicated relationship with money. And it starts early. Many families shield their daughters from anything related to ‘finance’, but this well-meaning protection ends up being a disservice. For when these young women step into the real world, they lack the confidence to make financial decisions - not because they’re incapable, but because they were never given information on budgeting, savings, and investments. Later in life, many continue to rely on their partners for investment decisions. This dependency on others will only stop when they have the right information and toolkit to take back control. That’s where we come in.We launch 'In Her Interest' on 22nd May in Delhi and will focus on one core idea for the launch:Building financial security for women.The session will be led by two amazing experts who truly get it.Zeba Zaidi Adeeb, Co-Founder - Spectacom Global, Creator of Devils Circuit & The Yoddha RaceManisha Pande, Tax Partner, Aeka Advisors (ex - GE, AA/EY)This session is on  Finance and the Entrepreneur.Once you buy a ticket, you also get to send a question that the speakers will address during the session.Note: Each session is theme-based, this session is about 'Finance and the Entrepreneur' - we are not talking stocks and shares.

name: Open SketchBook
description: Open SketchBook: You don’t need to leave the city to feel grounded.OverviewOur core belief is that you do not need to leave the city to feel grounded. In a world where everyone is distracted, we pause as a group to give our attention to one shared thing. Sketching becomes the medium to notice small, living details in urban spaces that we usually rush through.You do not need to be an artist to enjoy sketching. Open SketchBook is a session for anyone who wants to explore drawing as a way to relax and observe the world. It is about the process of putting pencil to paper rather than the final masterpiece.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community led initiative dedicated to creating safe and mindful spaces. name: #lecture31: How Not To Be Fooled By Data
description: India is often described through abstractions.A billion people. A growing economy. A young country. A democracy of dizzying complexity.But somewhere between these familiar descriptions lies another India. One hiding quietly inside numbers, maps, charts, and patterns we rarely stop to examine closely.The next 100 babies born in India will not arrive evenly across the country. Some languages are disappearing while others coexist within the same neighbourhood. Millions describe themselves as employed in ways economists struggle to classify. Entire assumptions about food habits collapse once data replaces perception.And sometimes, statistics reveal stories stranger than fiction.Data is often treated as cold, technical, and inaccessible. Yet the best data has always done something more ambitious. It helps us notice. It exposes contradictions, unsettles assumptions, and occasionally changes how we understand ourselves.This session invites you into that process of discovery.Through surprises, puzzles, myth-busting facts, and visually engaging charts, the lecture will move across questions that are at once playful and deeply revealing:Where will the next 100 babies in India be born? Which is India’s most multilingual city? Why is almost nobody in India considered jobless after 29? Why does a banker contribute far more to GDP than a farmer? What is the perception-versus-reality gap between vegetarian and non-vegetarian India? And are SRK’s and Rajinikanth’s future heroines even born yet?The evening will mix insight with curiosity, humour with analysis, and information with surprise. Expect twists, unexpected connections, and charts that feel less like presentations and more like treasure maps.At a moment when information is abundant but attention is fragmented, perhaps the rarest experience is learning to see familiar things differently.About the SpeakerRohit Saran is the Managing Editor of The Times of India. Over the course of his career, he has served as Executive Editor at The Times of India, The Economic Times, and India Today, and was previously Editor-in-Chief of Business Today and Khaleej Times.Since the 1990s, he has worked extensively in data and visual journalism, combining numbers, visuals, and narrative to build richer forms of storytelling across some of India’s leading newsrooms.Earlier this year, Rohit co-authored 100 Ways to See India - Stats, Stories and Surprises with Sajeev Kumarapuram, Design Editor at The Times of India. Together, they have spent years navigating India’s data landscape searching for elusive numbers, surviving endless PDFs, and uncovering stories hidden inside statistics.Book now & develop your own pint of view! 🍻📌 Note: Alcohol will be served only to guests aged 25 and above. Non-alcoholic options will be available. Attendance implies consent to being filmed for event documentation.

name: Surface and Memory: an image transfer workshop
description: Gallery XXL presents Surface and Memory, an image transfer workshop with artist Kapil Jangid (@streetdog0506), exploring materiality and the ways surfaces can hold memory, traces, and personal narratives.Rooted in Kapil’s practice engaging with architecture, urban landscapes, labour, and material exploration, the workshop will introduce participants to techniques of transferring photographic imagery onto surfaces such as MDF boards, ceramic tiles, glass, and other materials.Through guided exercises and experimentation, participants will learn how everyday materials can become sites for storytelling and visual intervention.As part of the workshop, each participant is required to submit one photograph prior to the session, which they will work with during the workshop.Ticket price: ₹1000 per participantDate: Saturday, May 23, 2026 Time: 5 pm - 6.30 pmVenue: Gallery XXL, A-277, Defence Colony, New Delhi, Delhi-110024*All materials will be provided at the venue*Please note there is limited parking available at the gallery; we recommend using public transportFor any further information, contact us at info@galleryxxl.com or +91 9324857354

name: When Power Claims Victimhood
description: Join us for an evening of unpacking how the language of equality is being reshaped in India’s courts, and what it means when power begins to claim victimhood.About the LectureWe often think of equality as a fixed principle, clear, neutral, and universally agreed upon. But what if the definition of equality itself is being redefined in real time?Drawing from landmark cases and ongoing constitutional debates, this lecture explores the idea of equality under the Indian Constitution and how it is being contested today. At its core is a provocative question, what happens when historically dominant groups begin to frame themselves as victims?Through a close reading of recent litigation, the session examines how legal arguments are reframing structural advantage as disadvantage, and how courts are becoming arenas where these competing narratives are negotiated.Moving across themes of caste, religion, gender, and institutional power, the lecture explores:Power and Victimhood: How dominant groups are increasingly adopting the language of historical injustice and reparationsEquality as Transformation: Why the Constitution envisioned equality not as neutrality, but as a tool to address deep-rooted structural inequalitiesThe Courtroom as Battleground: How contemporary legal disputes, from reservations to religious rights, are reshaping constitutional meaningReverse Discrimination Debates: The rise of arguments that position corrective measures as unfair to historically privileged groupsWhat’s at Stake: How these shifts could redefine the very foundation of India’s constitutional projectFlow of the Evening5:00 PM: Doors open at Social, Saket: Come in, find your seat, and get comfortable5:00 – 5:20 PM: Place your orders and start a few conversations5:30 PM: The learning begins6:15 PM: Open Q&A and discussion with Ms. Wadekar6:30 PM onwards: Stay a while: More eating, more drinking, more conversationsAbout the SpeakerMs. Disha Wadekar is a constitutional lawyer with over a decade of litigation experience in India. An LL.M. graduate from Columbia Law School and a Fulbright scholar, her work sits at the intersection of law, social justice, and structural inequality.She has been involved in several significant constitutional cases, including the Sabarimala temple entry case and the EWS reservation case. She has represented the families of Rohith Vemula and Payal Tadvi in their fight against institutional discrimination, as well as journalist Sukanya Shantha in a petition challenging caste-based division of labour in Indian prisons. In 2018, she was the only woman lawyer representing Dalit victims before the Bhima Koregaon Judicial Inquiry Commission.In 2022, she was appointed Additional Special Public Prosecutor in a case concerning the rape of a Dalit girl in Rajasthan.Beyond litigation, Ms. Wadekar has taught Constitutional Law and Critical Legal Studies at National Law University, Delhi and O.P. Jindal Global University. She is also the co-founder of CEDE (Community for the Eradication of Discrimination in Education and Employment), an organisation working to increase representation of marginalised communities in the legal profession and judiciary.Her work extends to legal research and consultancy with institutions such as the Centre for Women’s Development Studies and Project 39A, and her writing has been featured in leading journals and platforms.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: SCC Gourmet Cinema - Midnight in Paris
description: Sunset Cinema Club, in collaboration with The Piano Man New Delhi, invites you to a moveable feast through time, where nostalgia, romance, and timeless flavors transport you to a Paris that exists somewhere between memory and imagination.Inspired by Midnight in Paris, this experience follows Gil Pender’s whimsical journey through the streets of Paris, where past and present blur, and every midnight unlocks a different era. As he drifts from modern day Paris to the golden age of the 1920s, and further into the dreamlike charm of La Belle Époque, the film becomes an ode to art, longing, and the magic of another time.Experience this journey through a thoughtfully curated 5-course tasting menu inspired by Paris through time, from modern day Paris to the roaring 1920s, culminating in the elegance of La Belle Époque. name: Dilliwale- Some interesting characters of Delhi
description: Kathika is delighted to host an evocative  Baithak with historian Swapna Liddle, an acclaimed historian and author Swapna Liddle.  Titled “Dilliwale- Some interesting characters of Delhi” - Delhi has always been more than a city — it is a living mosaic of personalities, each leaving behind stories that shape its unique character. “Dilliwale – Some Interesting Characters of Delhi” is an invitation to step into this vibrant human tapestry, where history unfolds not just through events, but through the lives of those who inhabited its streets, courts, bazaars and neighbourhoods.In the intimate setting of a Baithak, historian Swapna Liddle will guide the audience through a curated selection of fascinating individuals from Delhi’s past — rulers and rebels, poets and patrons, ordinary citizens and eccentric figures — each offering a glimpse into the social and cultural fabric of their time.Through anecdotes, lesser-known narratives, and vivid storytelling, the session will explore what it meant to be a “Dilliwala” across different eras. From the refinement of courtly life to the energy of bustling bazaars, and from moments of great upheaval to everyday resilience, these stories collectively reveal the many moods and identities of the city.

name: The Mirror Image and sub(text): What Lies Beneath
description: Join us on Saturday, May 24, for an evening that begins with something deceptively familiar and follows it into questions of identity, fragmentation, desire, and the unsettling spaces beneath appearances.About the LectureA mirror appears generous. It reflects back what stands before it with near-perfect precision. And yet, something is always slightly off. This lecture begins with the simple act of looking at ourselves and asks what that reflection conceals as much as what it reveals. If the mirror produces an image that is almost identical, where does that subtle dissonance come from? What lies in the gap between ourselves and the image staring back at us?Drawing from visual culture, philosophy, psychoanalysis, and cultural theory, the session explores the strange and deeply human relationship we have with reflection, recognition, and selfhood.Moving across themes of identity, visuality, and the politics of perception, the lecture explores:The Mirror as Illusion: Why reflections feel complete even when they conceal fragmentation beneath the surface.The Gaps Within the Self: How mirrors provoke questions about the parts of ourselves that remain hidden, disowned, or unresolved.Image vs. Reality: The tension between carefully constructed identities and the “bits and pieces” that resist coherence.Visuality and Power: How seeing, and being seen, shapes subjectivity, desire, and social life.When the Mirror Cracks: Whether reflection offers reassurance, or marks the beginning of deeper anxieties about who we are.Because sometimes, the most unsettling thing is not what the mirror shows us, but what it leaves out.Flow of the Evening 4:00 PM: Doors open at Depot 48, GK II: Come in, find your seat, and get comfortable4:05 – 4:20 PM: Place your orders and start a few conversations4:30 PM: The learning begins!5:15 PM: Open Q&A and discussion with Prof. Gabriel5:30 PM onwards: Stay a while: More eating, more drinking, more conversationsAbout the SpeakerProfessor Karen Gabriel heads the Department of English at St. Stephen’s College, University of Delhi, and is the Founder-Director of the Centre for Gender, Culture and Social Processes—an academic space dedicated to transdisciplinary research and critical inquiry.Her work spans gender, sexuality, cinema, melodrama, nationalism, visuality, and representation, with a sustained interest in how images and technologies shape cultural and political life. She is the author of Melodrama and the Nation: Sexual Economies of Bombay Cinema 1970–2000 and editor of Gendered Nation, alongside numerous contributions to international handbooks, encyclopedias, and scholarly volumes.Professor Gabriel is currently working on a book on homosociality and continues to engage deeply with questions around visual culture and the politics of seeing.Her academic work has been recognised through several international fellowships and awards, including the Digital Fellowship at Columbia University, the Marie Curie Fellowship from the European Union, the Leverhulme Fellowship in the UK, and postdoctoral fellowships in Sweden focused on gender excellence. She was also the 2012 Scholar in Residence at the College of William and Mary in the United States.An interesting fact about her: she has a fondness for creatures that are often overlooked or feared, lizards, spiders, beetles, owls, and crows.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from St. Stephen’s, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: The Politics of Motherhood
description: Join us on Sunday, May 24, for a conversation that asks a question we’ve long internalised but rarely examined closely: What if the way we talk about mothers isn’t just cultural, but deeply political?About the LectureMotherhood is often framed as instinctive, natural, and deeply personal. But beneath these ideas lies a powerful and persistent narrative, one that places the burden of blame, responsibility, and perfection squarely on mothers.This session explores how motherhood has been shaped by cultural expectations that are often contradictory and impossible to meet. A mother is told she loves too much or too little, is too present or not present enough. Working mothers are scrutinised, while those who stay at home are diminished. Even the choice to not become a mother carries its own weight of judgment.At its core, this lecture reframes these experiences, not as individual failings, but as part of a larger system.The session explores:The Blame Narrative: How mothers have historically been positioned as responsible for outcomes beyond their controlCultural Contradictions: The impossible standards placed on women across different forms of motherhoodLanguage as Power: How psychological and cultural terms, from ‘mother wound’ to attachment styles, can unintentionally reinforce blameInternalised Shame: How these narratives shape the way mothers see themselves, and each otherRewriting the Story: What it means to view motherhood through a lens of context, care, and social structures rather than individual faultAbout the SpeakerDr. Shelja Sen is a Narrative & Family Therapist, writer, and Co-founder of Children First. She is a TED speaker, a columnist with The Indian Express, and the author of three critically acclaimed books: All You Need is Love, Imagine, and Reclaim Your Life.She serves as a Clinical Tutor at the University of Melbourne and is the Editor-in-Chief of the International Journal of Narrative Therapy & Community Work. Her work is rooted in building culturally aligned, ethical practices that bring together an intersectional lens, social justice, and innovation.At heart, she is a storyteller, someone deeply invested in how the stories we inherit shape the lives we live.All Grounds for Thought tickets include a ₹250 coupon redeemable on food and beverages at the venue.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Why Did Bengal Vote Differently? | Policy & Chai
description: Every month, we pick one topic — a policy decision, a political shift, a global event — and actually talk it through. No lectures, no expertise required. Just curious people, different perspectives, and chai.This month's question: Why does Bengal vote differently from the rest of India?From the Left Front's 34-year reign to Mamata's TMC dominance, Bengal has consistently defied national political trends. What makes it so distinct — is it history, identity, class politics, or something else entirely? And what does it mean for 2026?What to expectA moderated discussion where everyone gets to speak. We go through different layers of the topic — historical context, current dynamics, what's at stake — and open it up for the room to debate, disagree, and figure things out together.No slides. No panel. Just a good conversation.Who's it forAnyone who's curious about Indian politics, follows the news, or just wants a space to think out loud with people who take these things seriously. You don't need to know Bengal politics inside out — half the fun is figuring it out together in the room.Join the communityWant to stay in the loop for future sessions? Join the Policy & Chai WhatsApp community here: https://chat.whatsapp.com/KHdTLqcmEaz53DBy1gSsB6Details📅 Sunday, 24th May⏰ 5:00 PM onwards📍 Siyah Art House, New Delhi🎟️ ₹219

name: #lecture32: How to Map a Revolution: AI & the DNA
description: Why do groups fail to act together, even when their interests align?This lecture explores the hidden dynamics of human coordination, from small teams to entire nations, and why breakdowns are more common than we assume. Drawing on behavioral science, geopolitics, and the study of complex systems, it looks at how trust, emotional bonds, and misaligned incentives shape collective action.The talk unfolds in three parts.Part 1: It begins with the foundations of coordination, examining the psychological and structural conditions that allow individuals and groups to move in sync.Part 2: It then shifts to the geopolitics of emotional bonding, exploring how identity, belonging, and narrative influence decisions at national and global scales.Part 3: Finally, through case studies from regions such as Iran and India, it traces how coordination failures appear in the real world and what they reveal about emerging global patterns.By the end, attendees will have a clearer understanding of why coordination breaks down even among actors who seem aligned, how emotions and identity quietly drive large-scale decisions, and how to better anticipate and respond to these failures across systems, organizations, and societies.Event details:Fort City Brewing, Hauz Khas MarketWednesday, 27 May| 7 PM to 9 PMWhat your ticket includes:1 optional complimentary beer or a mocktailSeating will be on first-come, first-served basis.What to Expect:A 60-minute lecture30–45 minutes of interactive Q&ASocial mixer and personal Q&As with the lecturerAbout the LecturerDr. Raul V. Rodriguez focuses on a critical & increasingly urgent problem: how to empirically measure and understand the ways in which minds, institutions, and societies fail or thrive under conditions of overload, AI pressure, and leadership stress. His research is driven by a unifying question across domains such as elections, markets, organizations, and infrastructure systems: how intelligence and rationality can be formally modeled before the conditions that sustain them begin to degrade.As Vice President of Woxsen University and Sir Cary Cooper Professor of Organizational Behaviour, he combines direct institutional engagement with his work as a computational social scientist modeling systemic instability.About Pint of ViewInspired by the successful Lectures on Tap community in the U.S., Pint of View brings this format to India for the first time. It’s where big ideas meet bar tables, and where you enjoy a drink in hand, walking out with a new way of seeing the world.Book now & develop your own pint of view! 🍻

name: Period Party 2.0 Delhi
description: Period Party is a deliberately intimate, community-led gathering held on World Menstrual Hygiene Day (May 28), designed to reclaim menstruation as a topic of open conversation, celebration, and solidarity. Period Party 2.0— a radical, unapologetic, bold, joyful, and inclusive gathering that reimagines how we talk, learn, and feel about our bodies and menstruation. Through creativity, shared stories, and reflection, we hope to create a space that feels safe, supportive, and stigma-free. Expect an insightful panels, a reflective workshop, sharing circles, poetry and collective feminist art. It is something you have never witnessed before!

name: Table of the Levant
description: Table of the Levant is an intimate Eastern Mediterranean supper club in Delhi, built around a long, shared table and a menu inspired by the kitchens of the Levant.We’ll start with a lemon–mint Polo Damascus welcome drink before moving into a generous mezze spread – hummus, mutabbal, labneh, fattoush, Cypriot-style corn, kefta and falafel, with warm pita and plenty of olives and pickles.For the main course, we’ll serve a dramatic Maklouba – a layered, spiced rice and vegetable dish flipped out at the table – followed by a classic ekmek dessert to finish on something sweet and comforting.Seats are limited, the menu is fixed, and the evening is designed for unhurried conversation, shared plates, and a sense of travelling across the Levant without leaving Delhi.

name: Clay, Conversation & Sangria
description: Step into an afternoon of Clay creativity, conversation, and Sangria by V's Supper Stories!A slow evening of pottery, sangria, nibbles, and meaningful conversation.Join us for an intimate golden-hour gathering where you’ll create and handpaint your own ceramic bowl in a guided pottery session designed for complete beginners and curious creatives alike.Spend the evening shaping clay, painting your pieces with your own creativity, sipping sangria, grazing on high tea bites, and meeting people around a shared table.Date - Saturday, 30th May 2026Time - 4:30pm - 6:30pm Your session includes:• Guided pottery experience by Mitti and Mind• One handcrafted ceramic bowl• Painting and creative detailing• Nibbles and Sangria • Glazing and firing of your final pieceOnce the firing process is complete, your finished bowls will be ready for pickup approximately 2 weeks after the event.No experience needed,  just bring yourself.*Please note: Handmade pottery is naturally imperfect, and that’s what makes each piece unique. name: Why We Taste With Our Ears
description: This lecture promises to be an evening that rethinks something as simple as eating, by tuning to a sense we rarely notice at the table.About the LectureWhy does the sound of rain make fried food more irresistible? Why does music change how we experience a glass of wine?This session begins with a simple but surprising idea: taste doesn’t start in the mouth, it often begins in the ear.Drawing on extensive research by Bruite Magazine across the subcontinent, this lecture explores how sound quietly shapes the way we anticipate, experience, and remember food. From the rhythm of a bustling street to the curated playlists of a fine-dining restaurant, auditory cues influence everything from perceived texture to indulgence and value.Moving across everyday eating environments and contemporary food spaces, the session explores:The Sound of Anticipation: How auditory cues shape cravings even before the first biteSonic Texture: Why crunch, sizzle, and silence can alter how we perceive flavour and freshnessAtmosphere & Memory: How music, noise, and rhythm influence mood and the way meals stay with usDesigning Food Experiences: The role of sound in restaurants, bars, supper clubs, and immersive dining formatsBlending cultural observation, field research, and sharp storytelling, this session offers a fresh lens on something deeply familiar.Flow of the Evening5:00 PM: Doors open at Siyah Arthouse, Saket: Come in, find your seat, and get comfortable5:00 – 5:20 PM: Place your orders and start a few conversations5:30 PM: The learning begins6:15 PM: Open Q&A and discussion with Ms. Ramya6:30 PM onwards: Stay a while: More eating, more drinking, more conversationsAbout the SpeakerRamya G Sri is the Co-founder and Editor-in-Chief of Bruite Magazine, one of South Asia’s leading independent food and culture publications known for its deeply researched, original storytelling and on-ground reporting.Through Bruite, she collaborates with chefs, historians, artisans, and communities across the subcontinent to explore how food intersects with culture, memory, and perception—often through unexpected and underexplored lenses.She also co-leads Bruite Media Co., a specialised brand advisory that has worked with over 500 brands and founders across F&B, lifestyle, D2C, and consumer tech. Her work spans brand launches, communication strategy, and creative training—shaping how brands tell stories and connect with audiences.Her contributions to storytelling have been recognised with the Laadli Media Award for Gender-Sensitive Storytelling and the Abby Silver Award for Advertising. Across her work, she brings together rigorous research, editorial depth, and a strong instinct for how narrative shapes both culture and consumer experience.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Unscript 2.0: Improv Comedy Show
description: After 14 hours of high-energy, no-holds-barred workshopping, this ensemble—now working at Level 2—steps into performance with sharper instincts, bolder choices, and a deepened sense of play. What unfolds is completely unscripted, wildly unpredictable, and built in the momenThis isn’t just a show you watch. It’s one you fuel.Your suggestions spark scenes. Your ideas twist the story. Your laughter drives the rhythm.A single word can spiral into a love story, a disaster, or something beautifully absurd. One performer holds the thread, the rest run with it—and somehow, chaos turns into magic.No scripts. No second takes. Just quick thinking, deep listening, and fearless fun.Expect moments that land perfectly, moments that go hilariously sideways, and everything in between. That’s where the real joy lives.HighlightsFully improvised, unscripted comedyAudience participation shapes the showPerformed by Level 2 trained ensembleHigh-energy, interactive experienceEvery show is completely uniqueAbout the HostAnshu DagaAn improv facilitator and performer guiding immersive, high-energy workshops that focus on spontaneity, presence, and creative expression.Come for the spontaneity. name: Overthinkers Anonymous
description: OverviewEver wondered why you default to people pleasing, perfectionism, or avoidance? Overthinkers Anonymous is a small and guided space to slow down, reflect, and talk honestly about the things that occupy our minds. This session focuses on Family Patterns and how the environments we grew up in still shape how we think, feel, and respond today.We focus on connecting the dots between past experiences and present reactions in a safe and judgment free environment. The goal is to build awareness and self compassion rather than focusing on blame.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community-led initiative dedicated to creating safe, mindful spaces. Through workshops and gatherings, we help individuals navigate life’s complexities, from mental well-being to personal growth, with empathy and shared human connection.What to expectPattern Recognition: Explore why tendencies like overthinking or control keep showing up in your life.Guided Reflection: Use a specialized worksheet to gain context on your reactions without the weight of self-judgment.Awareness & Compassion: A gentle space to build self-awareness and understanding—focused on insight, not on reliving trauma.Details🗓️ Date: 31st May (Sunday)⏰ Time: 11:30 AM📍 Location: Kanchanjunga Towers🎟️ Contribution: ₹700 (Includes Coffee + Snacks)

location.address: A-BLOCK, KANCHANJUNGA APARTMENT, G.H.18, near Post Office, Sector 56, Gurugram, Haryana 122011, India

name: Whose Art Is It Anyway?
description: Who gets to create, define, and own art?About the LectureArt is often spoken about as something universal, but in reality, art has never existed outside systems of access.This participatory lecture explores how artists from historically marginalised communities navigate spaces where their work is frequently appropriated, diluted, celebrated without context, or dismissed altogether. Drawing from performance and narrative traditions across South Asia, the session asks what shapes artistic legitimacy — and who gets excluded from it.The lecture explores:How caste, class, gender, religion, and sexuality influence who is allowed to create and whose work is valuedWhy certain forms of expression become “mainstream,” while others are labelled “folk,” “obscene,” or “political”How art can function both as a site of erasure and a tool of assertionWhat it means to witness, consume, and engage with art responsiblyThrough examples, discussion, and interactive prompts, participants will reflect on the relationship between art, memory, dignity, and justice.At its core, this lecture invites us to see art not as passive representation, but as a way of negotiating identity, resistance, and belonging.About the SpeakerSri Vamsi Matta is a Bangalore-based interdisciplinary theatre artist whose work is rooted in lived experience, political memory, and anti-caste thought. Born into a Dalit community, his practice engages with everyday and structural violence of caste, positioning art as a space for refusal, repair, and collective imagination.Working across theatre, performance, writing, and participatory formats, his work brings together autobiography, community history, and radical pedagogy. His recent works include Star in the Sky, exploring the emotional and political lives of Dalit students in Indian universities, and Come Eat With Me, a participatory performance examining caste through food and shared meals, staged across India, the United States, and Australia.Vamsi has facilitated workshops, lectures, and teaching engagements across institutions including Azim Premji University, Ashoka University, Northwestern University, and the University of Texas at Austin, bringing anti-caste artistic practice into conversation with diverse audiences nationally and internationally.All Grounds for Thought tickets include a ₹250 coupon redeemable on food and beverages at the venue.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: The Blue Moon Sound Bath
description: A rare celestial event. A shared energy. A real shift. The Immersion Room in collaboration with Eka by Paayal invites you into an immersive Blue Moon Sound Bath — a deeply calming evening designed to help you slow down, release emotional heaviness, and reconnect with yourself. Inspired by the symbolic energy of the Blue Moon, this experience becomes a gentle space for reflection, renewal, and inner stillness.As you arrive, you’ll be guided through grounding breathwork, intention setting, and a soothing meditation before sinking into a live sound immersion using healing instruments like sound bowls and chimes. Leave feeling lighter.
